front: Poem: a musician plays a 'drowsy syncopated tune' 'down on Lenox Avenue' by 'the pale dull pallor of an old gas light'; afterward sleeps 'like a rock or a man that's dead'
Poem: 'My soul has grown deep like the rivers'; speaker 'bathed in the Euphrates' and 'heard the singing of the Mississippi when Abe Lincoln went down to New Orleans'
back: The Negro Speaks of Rivers (Langston Hughes)

front: Poem: 'What happens to a dream deferred? / Does it dry up / like a raisin in the sun?'; inspired Lorraine Hansberry's play title
Poem: speaker is 'the darker brother' sent to 'eat in the kitchen when company comes' but says 'Tomorrow, I'll be at the table'; responds to Whitman's 'I Hear America Singing'
Poem: a mother tells her son 'Life for me ain't been no crystal stair' with 'tacks in it / And splinters / And boards torn up'
Poem: 'I am the only colored student in my class' who likes 'a pipe for a Christmas present, / or records -- Bessie, bop, or Bach'
Author: wrote 'The Negro Artist and the Racial Mountain' in response to Schuyler's 'The Negro-Art Hokum'
Author: collaborated with Zora Neale Hurston on the play Mule Bone before their falling-out
Author: created the character Jesse B. Semple (Simple) for satirical stories
Author: translated Gabriela Mistral, Nicolas Guillen, and Garcia Lorca's Gypsy Ballads
Poem: composed on a train trip to Mexico; recounted in autobiography The Big Sea
back: The Negro Speaks of Rivers (Langston Hughes)

front: Poem: asks 'Say, who are you that mumbles in the dark?' and 'Who said the free? Not me?'
back: Let America Be America Again (Langston Hughes)

front: Author: novel set in 1910s Kansas featuring bluesman W.C. Handy adored by Hagar and Sandy
Poem: musician plays with 'his ebony hands on each ivory key' making the piano 'moan with melody'
Author: produced the photobook The Sweet Flypaper of Life with photographer Roy DeCarava
